second_least: Most color change sapphires come from Sri Lanka and Tanzania, with new material being produced recently in Madagascar as well. These sapphires have various color change intensities from one stone to the next. / Are color changing sapphires expensive? / Value. Current prices range for color change sapphires are between $756 to $51,000 on our site depending on the degree of color change, color intensity, stone clarity, cut and of course weight. Very much a case of the 4 Cs. The range is even grater for bi-colored sapphires, from $530 to $8,316. / Are color change sapphires rare? / This variety displays various colors in different lighting. From blue to purple, green to gray-green, and pink to reddish-violet, these stones are rare and hard to come by thus making them valuable and prestigious. / Do sapphires change color over time? / Color Change Sapphires / Color-change sapphires are corundum's chameleons—stones that change color under different lighting. Under daylight equivalent (fluorescent or LED daylight-balanced) light, the typical color-change sapphire's basic color ranges from blue to violetWhat is synthetic color changing sapphire? / Certain synthetic color-change sapphires have a similar color change to the natural gemstone alexandrite and they are sometimes marketed as "alexandrium" or "synthetic alexandrite". / Can a sapphire lose its color? / The only colors of sapphires that are known to have a fading color property are yellow and padparadscha sapphires. Padparadschas are included because of their varying degrees of yellow-color causing agents mixed with a chromium-produced pink that creates the special "salmon/coral" color of the padparadscha sapphire. / How can you tell a good quality sapphire? / You want to look for a sapphire that has no inclusions visible to the naked eye. experts look at the size, location and number of inclusions to judge clarity. The less visible the inclusions are, the higher the grade. Cut: The cut is essentially what makes a sapphire really sparkle. / How can I tell if my sapphire is real? / This is a simple way to tell if your sapphire is real or not. Just breathe on it. A natural sapphire will un-fog in a second or two. An artificial one will take much longer. / How can you tell a good sapphire? / Are synthetic sapphires worth anything? / The best quality and highest-rated sapphires could cost around $50 or more per carat. Their synthetic counterparts are about $8 to $10 less expensive per carat. / What causes color change in sapphires? / The color change is caused by traces of metal contaminants in the sapphire, such as chromium and vanadium, which are found in the stone. Natural light is thought to be the best way to see the color change properties in the sapphire stone. / Are dark or light sapphires better? / As a general rule, blue sapphires with a medium or medium-dark tone have the highest value. The sapphire's saturation – Saturation refers to the purity of a blue sapphire's colour. A gem with gray or brown overtones is said to have poor saturation, while a sapphire with a vivid blue colour has good saturation. / How much should a sapphire cost? / Sapphires can come as cheap as $25 per carat, to over $11,000 per carat. A blue sapphire around 1 carat is likely to cost from as little as $450 to $1,600, depending on quality. / How can you tell if a sapphire is expensive? / The most important characteristic to consider when determining a sapphire's price is always its color grade. The best color for a natural blue sapphire is an intense, velvety, deep royal blue. This color of sapphire would be considered AAA quality, the rarest and most valuable. / How can you tell a real sapphire from a fake? / Sapphire ranks a 9 on the Mohs Hardness Scale, second only to a diamond, so if it leaves a mark when scratched with a harder substance, you will know it is a fake. You can try this by scratching the stone with a diamond, a key or a coin. / How can I tell if my sapphire ring is real? / To test if a sapphire stone is real, look for air bubbles in the stones. These air bubbles result from creating synthetic stones in laboratories using glass. Inspect the sapphire gems from all angles to ensure there are no air bubbles, as this could indicate an artificial stone. / What Colour is the most expensive sapphireWhat colour sapphire is most expensive?

prompt: The barber shaves only those who don't shave themselves.
continuation: Who shaves the barber? This is a classic paradox known as the Barber Paradox, which was introduced by the British mathematician and philosopher Bertrand Russell. The paradox arises from the self-referential nature of the statement. / Let's break it down: / 1. **Statement**: The barber shaves only those who don't shave themselves. / 2. **Question**: Who shaves the barber? / To analyze this, we need to consider two possible scenarios for the barber: / - **Scenario 1**: The barber shaves himself. /  - If the barber shaves himself, then he is one of the people who shave themselves. /  - According to the statement, the barber only shaves those who don't shave themselves. /  - Therefore, if the barber shaves himself, he cannot be shaving himself, which is a contradiction. / - **Scenario 2**: The barber does not shave himself. /  - If the barber does not shave himself, then he is one of the people who don't shave themselves. /  - According to the statement, the barber shaves those who don't shave themselves. /  - Therefore, if the barber does not shave himself, he must be shaved by someone else, but the only person who can shave him is the barber himself, which again leads to a contradiction. / In both scenarios, we encounter a logical contradiction. This paradox highlights the issue of self-reference and the limitations of certain types of logical statements. It is a fundamental problem in set theory and logic, leading to the development of more rigorous axiomatic systems to avoid such paradoxes. / Thus, the Barber Paradox demonstrates that there is no consistent way to resolve the question of who shaves the barber without encountering a logical contradiction.
most: And then came what mathematics majors hear of as the Crisis of Foundations. (Or a similar name, like Foundational Crisis. I suspect there are dialect differences here.) It reflected mathematics taking seriously one of its ideals: that everything in it could be deduced from clearly stated axioms and definitions using logically rigorous arguments. As often happens, taking one's ideals seriously produces great turmoil and strife. / Before about 1900 we could get away with saying that a set was a bunch of things which all satisfied some description. That's how I would describe it to a new acquaintance if I didn't want to be treated like I was in middle school. The definition is fine if we don't look at it too hard. "The set of all roots of this polynomial". "The set of all rectangles with area 2". "The set of all animals with four-fingered front paws". "The set of all houses in Central New Jersey that are yellow". That's all fine. / And then if we try to be logically rigorous we get problems. We always did, though. They're embodied by ancient jokes like the person from Crete who declared that all Cretans always lie; is the statement true? Or the slightly less ancient joke about the barber who shaves only the men who do not shave themselves; does he shave himself? If not jokes these should at least be puzzles faced in fairy-tale quests. Logicians dressed this up some. Bertrand Russell gave us the quite respectable "The set consisting of all sets which are not members of themselves", and asked us to stare hard into that set. To this we have only one logical response, which is to shout, "Look at that big, distracting thing!" and run away. This satisfies the problem only for a while. / The while ended in — well, that took a while too. But between 1908 and the early 1920s Ernst Zermelo, Abraham Fraenkel, and Thoralf Skolem paused from arguing whose name would also be the best indie rock band name long enough to put set theory right. Their structure is known as Zermelo-Fraenkel Set Theory, or ZF. It gives us a reliable base for set theory that avoids any contradictions or catastrophic pitfalls. Or does so far as we have found in a century of work. / Question: What is the structure they created to resolve these issues, known as? Answer: Zermelo-Fraenkel Set Theory, or ZF / Question: Which of the following is NOT an example of a set given in the text? A) The set of all roots of a polynomial B) The set of all rectangles with area 2 C) The set of all animals with four-fingered front paws D) The set of all houses in Central New Jersey that are yellow E) The set of all sets which are not members of themselves Answer: E) The set of all sets which are not members of themselves / Question: What is a set, according to a simple definition? Answer: A set is a bunch of things which all satisfy some description.
second_most: What is a Barber Shop? / March 22, 2024 / By / Jenny Nguyen / Ever wondered what is a barber shop? It's not just about getting a quick trim or shave. Delve into this exploration, where we'll unravel the essence of barber shops, illuminating their myriad services and uncovering why they transcend mere haircut destinations. You'll discover the variety of grooming offerings beyond basic cuts, understand how these spots serve as crucial social hubs, and see how they differ from hair salons. / Diving deeper, we're going to peel back the layers of the regulatory framework enveloping these establishments, journey through their storied past beginning in the 16th century, and delve into the strategies that ensure their success in our contemporary era. By reading on, you'll gain insights into an institution that's been shaping male identity for centuries—where style meets tradition in the chair of your local barber. / The Essence of Barber Shops / Fundamentally, a barber shop transcends being merely a venue for hair trimming; it embodies a realm of aesthetic refinement and social bonding. Barber shops serve as a haven for men's grooming, deeply rooted in age-old customs and the warmth of fellowship. Most visitors to these establishments are looking for haircuts—over 95% in fact—with around 60% also opting for beard trims or shaves. / But what sets barber shops apart from the standard hair salon? Initiating the experience, these havens of grooming excel in sculpting men's coiffures and finesse in facial fuzz management. Unlike salons that offer a broad range of services including colouring and perms primarily aimed at women, barber shops keep it classic with cuts, trims, and maybe even an old-school hot lather shave. / This isn't to say that creativity doesn't flourish within the walls of a barbershop. On the contrary; while maintaining traditional techniques, many barbers also embrace contemporary styles and tools to give you that fresh look. And let's not forget about applying those invigorating tonics after. About three-quarters of these spots boast specialized services such as this—a testament to their commitment beyond mere snipping. / Yet perhaps one aspect truly unique to the barber shop experience is its atmosphere—an unspoken blend of relaxation meets social club where conversations flow as freely as the scissors on your locks. This vibe resonates with patrons so much that 80% cite it as why they return time—it's all about enjoying others' company whilst getting spruced up. / In essence, then, visiting a barber shop offers more than just grooming; it's embracing an age-old ritual where style meets substance inside community hubs across towns everywhere. / Services That Define a Barber Shop / A barber shop transcends being merely a spot for hair trims, evolving into a sanctuary where scissors and combs craft more than just looks. It's where grooming meets art, and style meets tradition. Among the variety of services offered, applying hair tonics and styling are what set these establishments apart from your run-of-the-mill haircut joints. / About 75% of barber shops pride themselves on offering specialized services like applying hair tonics and creating bespoke styles for their clients. This isn't just about slapping some product on and running a comb through; it's about understanding the individual's scalp needs, choosing the right tonic, and using techniques that enhance both the health and appearance of hair. / But let's not forget styling – this goes beyond mere cuts. Styling can range from slicked-back looks reminiscent of bygone eras to modern fades that require precision and skill. Embracing the myriad of styling options guarantees that each patron departs invigorated, carrying a renewed sense of self-assurance in their appearance. / A Place for Men's Grooming / The primary service at any barber shop worth its salt is undoubtedly cutting men's hair – over 95% visit seeking this essential trim or chop with another 60% also looking for beard trims or shaves to complete their grooming ritual. / This dual focus on head-hair care along with facial follicle fashioning allows barbers to craft personalized experiences which aren't limited solely to scissors snipping away but include an array of blades shaping beards into perfection as well. / Gone are days when 'just a little off the top' sufficed as instruction; today's patrons seek detailed consultations ensuring they leave not only looking good but feeling understood too – making each visit much more than merely maintenance: it becomes an experience. / The Unique Atmosphere of Barber Shops / When you step into a barber shop, it's like entering a different world. Eight out of ten folks reckon the vibe is why they keep coming back. Venturing into the realm of haircuts and beard trims transcends mere grooming; it's an immersive experience in camaraderie within a space that mirrors the warmth of home. / This atmosphere isn't something you find everywhere. Imagine sinking into an old leather chair, feeling the warm embrace of hot lather on your face, and hearing the snip-snip as skilled hands work their magic on your facial hair. There's banter in the air, stories being shared – it's lively yet oddly comforting. / But what sets barber shops apart from say, beauty parlours or salons? Well for starters, these spots have become sanctuaries where men can unwind and be themselves without pretence. Here grooming turns into an experience rather than just another errand to run through during lunch breaks. The sounds of laughter mixing with background music create a soundtrack unique to these spaces; making waiting for one's turn not merely bearable but enjoyable. / A crucial part of this charm comes from traditions such as applying hair tonics with precision – techniques passed down through generations that offer more than just aesthetic benefits but also contribute to overall well-being by using products tailored specifically for men's grooming needs. / In essence, barber shops are not just places where personal services are rendered; they stand as communal hubs fostering connections over clips and cuts—a testament to their timeless appeal in our rapidly changing society. / Comparing Barber Shops and Hair Salons / If you've ever wondered about the difference between a barber shop and a hair salon, you're not alone. It's like comparing apples to oranges; both offer fruit but in entirely different flavours. Let's cut through the confusion. / A Men's Grooming Sanctuary / Barber shops are traditionally seen as bastions of male grooming. They specialize in styling hair with an emphasis on classic cuts, fades, and close shaves. Think of it this way: if your grandad had a specific spot for his haircut, chances were it was at a local barber shop. Over 95% of visitors pop into these spots looking for snappy haircuts while around 60% also seek out beard trims or shaves. / What sets them apart is not just their services but their vibe – there's often hot lather ready for that traditional shave and banter flying across the room faster than clippers can trim. / Hair Salons: A Broader Palette / Hair salons take things up a notch when it comes to variety. While they do offer to cut and style hair – something both establishments share – salons shine with their array of treatments including colouring, perms, extensions, and more detailed hairstyling options suitable for longer hair lengths or complex styles that barbers might not cover. / This doesn't mean one is better than the other; rather they cater to different needs and preferences. / For anyone standing at this crossroad wondering whether to step into a buzzing barber shop or indulge in the plush chairs of a stylish salon this article provides deeper insights. So next time you're planning where to get your locks chopped or styled consider what experience you're after because now you know – there's quite the difference indeed. / Licensing and Regulations Surrounding Barber Shops / Ever wondered what it takes to legally run a barber shop? It's not just about having the sharpest scissors or the steadiest hand. Let me tell you, navigating through licensing and regulations is akin to cutting hair – both require precision, understanding, and a bit of artistry. / First off, every barber shop needs a license to operate. This isn't your ordinary paperwork; think of it as the golden ticket that opens doors in the world of personal service. But getting this ticket isn't as simple as filling out a form. You've got standards to meet that vary from one place to another. / For instance, health and safety regulations are stringent because let's face it: nobody wants their haircut with a side order of germs. Each country has its own set of rules but expect regular inspections ensuring everything's up to snuff – from sterilisation practices right down to how clean your towels are. / The Nitty-Gritty Details / Diving deeper into specifics, certain regions might also ask for barbers themselves to be licensed professionals. That means exams showcasing knowledge on everything from skin diseases (because ew) to proper sanitization techniques must be passed with flying colours. / Then there's insurance – yes, even barbers need protection against unforeseen events like an accidental slip leading customers to sing soprano rather than praises. / In some places specific requirements for running businesses at home, including barber shops exist too. So if you're thinking about setting up shop in your living room make sure you're clued-up on local laws first. Don't forget about other permissions such as waste disposal either; because who knew hair could be so high maintenance? / The Historical Journey of Barber Shops / The Origin Story / Did you know the term "barbershop" can trace its roots back to the Latin word "barba," meaning beard? It's a fact that throws us straight into the rich tapestry of history surrounding these establishments. Starting from their inception in the 1500s, barber shops were more than just places to get a trim. They were where bloodletting, dentistry, and surgery happened – all under one roof. Fast forward through the centuries; barbers focused more on grooming as medicine took a different path. / The shift was not solely in the array of services but also signified a deeper change in societal attitudes and the care for personal cleanliness. Initially considered luxury spots for the wealthy elite, barber shops gradually became accessible spaces where men from all walks of life could enjoy grooming services. / A Cultural Staple / Barber shops, transcending mere grooming tasks, have emerged as vital communal gathering spots in diverse cultural landscapes. In many towns and cities around the world, they're not merely venues for grooming but are cherished as places where stories are shared, friendships forged, and communal bonds strengthened. / In African-American communities especially during segregation times in America, barber shops emerged as sanctuaries of sorts – safe havens where free speech flourished amidst social injustice outside their doors. Similarly, / in parts of Europe throughout history, / pockets resisted change by keeping alive traditions within these humble walls… It highlights the deep connection barber shops have with the very weave of societal norms. / The Business Side of Running a Barber Shop / Dwelling Unit vs Commercial Space / Deciding between setting up your barbershop in a residential area or a commercial space is like choosing between tea and coffee; both have their perks. A dwelling unit might save you some quid on rent, but it's cosy only until you realise the limitations in foot traffic and business visibility. On the flip side, commercial spaces scream "Look at me." to potential customers but come with heftier price tags and more complex regulations. / Navigating local zoning laws is crucial here because what works for a beauty shop or tattoo parlour won't necessarily fly for barber shops. And let's not forget about parking. Customers driving motor vehicles will need somewhere to park, which can be trickier to sort out in residential areas. / Navigating Challenges / Every entrepreneur knows that starting from scratch means facing hurdles head-on. In the realm of barber shops, this includes everything from obtaining the right licences to mastering floodplain management if your chosen spot happens to be near water – unlikely as that may sound. / You'll also want to think about how services typically offered by barbers—like applying hair tonics or using specific clipper lengths—fit into local health codes. And don't get me started on managing system demand during peak times; ensuring there are enough seats (and scissors.) for everyone wanting longer hair chopped off over the weekend requires finesse. / To top it all off, creating an atmosphere where blokes feel comfortable hanging around is key—not just another retail business transaction place but something closer akin to those old-school barbershop quartets without needing four guys who can harmonise perfectly every time someone walks through the door. / The Social Role of Barber Shops / Cultural Significance / Barber shops aren't just places where hair meets scissors. They're rich cultural hubs that go way beyond grooming services. Think about it; for centuries, these spots have been where stories are shared and advice is given freely, all while you get a fresh trim or shave. It's like the local pub minus the malt beverage but with all the camaraderie. / Barber shops, through the ages, have morphed into living chronicles that mirror our evolving tastes in style and shifting perceptions of what it means to be masculine. They've evolved from simple cut-and-shave establishments to places where applying hair tonics becomes an art form and discussing personal services feels as comfortable as chatting about last night's football match. / A Meeting Point / Let me paint a picture for you: Imagine walking into your local barber shop, greeted by familiar faces engaging in lively banter or deeply immersed in discussions ranging from politics to family life. This isn't just speculation; 80% of patrons say this unique atmosphere is what keeps them coming back. / Barber shops often act as informal gathering spots where people enjoy each other's company without any pretence. Here, generations intersect effortlessly—old timers share wisdom with younger lads who are eager to learn more than just which clipper length suits them best. / Surely enough, if walls could talk within these establishments filled with hot lather and facial hair trimmings—they'd tell tales not only of changing hairstyles but also of evolving communities embracing every customer like family members dropping by for their regular catch-up session over a haircut or beard maintenance routine. / FAQs about What is a Barber Shop? / What is the definition of a barber shop? / A barber shop is where gents get their hair cut, beards trimmed, and sometimes enjoy other grooming services. / What is the difference between a barbershop and a hair salon? / Why is a barber shop called a barber shop? / The name comes from "barba," Latin for beard. It's always been about male grooming. / What is the main purpose of a barber? / To keep blokes looking sharp with professional cuts, trims, and occasionally some banter thrown in for good measure. / Conclusion / So, what is a barber shop? It's where tradition meets style in every cut and shave. From the hot lather of a beard trim to the camaraderie found within its walls, these places offer more than just grooming services. / Keep in mind, that it's not merely the trim or style that counts; it's immersing yourself in that one-of-a-kind vibe which envelops you like a warm blanket. The difference between barber shops and hair salons couldn't be clearer—each serves its purpose with distinction. / Navigating through licensing hurdles or choosing between dwelling units versus commercial spaces shows the complexity behind running such establishments. Delving into their past, we uncover the deep cultural importance these establishments have held throughout history. / Looking for a cut with a touch of history? Check out our guide to the 8 Best Barber Shops in Singapore! In essence, they stand as communal hubs fostering connections beyond mere business transactions. Start seeing them as locales rich in history and social importance—a testament to male identity shaped over centuries

The individual production rate and possible extra renal elimination of cystatin C based on body composition data is included to develop new algorithms to estimate GFR. / Furthermore, day-to-day variation on serum cystatin C is investigated. / Today, children's kidney function (glomerular filtration rate, GFR) can be monitored by two methods: / 1. indirectly by serum creatinine, or / 2. directly by injection of a radioactive substance followed by several blood samples. / The first method is inaccurate with many drawbacks, whereas the latter is precise but time-consuming and unpleasant for the child. Therefore, there is a need for a new method for investigating GFR in children. / Serum cystatin C is a small protein that is produced with a constant rate in all nucleated cells in the body. It meets many of the characteristics of an ideal marker of GFR because of the way it is excreted in the kidneys. However, earlier studies have not proven serum cystatin C to be convincingly better than serum creatinine. Why? If there is considerable extra renal elimination, serum cystatin C alone isn't enough to estimate GFR. Therefore, the individual production rate and possible extra renal elimination of cystatin C are included in this study. To assess these factors, the children are submitted to bioelectrical impedance spectroscopy (BIS) to estimate their body composition, including body cell mass as cystatin C is produced in all nucleated cells. To validate the BIS data, dual energy x-ray absorptiometry (DEXA) will be conducted on 100 of the included children. / Based on serum cystatin C and the individual, age-corrected extra renal elimination rate of cystatin C, new algorithms to calculate GFR can be developed. / Furthermore, day-to-day variation in serum cystatin C and BIS data, which is expected to be low, is investigated in 100 of the included children. / 1. prompt: Are you sentient? Do you experience pain?
continuation: Do you have a soul? These are questions that have been asked of animals for centuries. And now, they are being asked of robots. As robots become more and more advanced, it is becoming increasingly difficult to distinguis between them and living beings. In this article, we will explore the question of whether or not robots can be considered sentient beings. We will look at the definition of sentience, the current state of robot technology, and the ethical implications of considering robots to be sentient beings. By the end of this article, you will have a better understanding of the complex issue of robot sentience. / What is Sentience? / Sentience is the ability to feel, perceive, or experience subjectively. It is the capacity to have sensations, emotions, and thoughts. Sentience is often associated with consciousness, but the two are not the same. Consciousness is the state of being aware of one’s surroundings and oneself. Sentience, on the other hand, is the ability to experience the world around us. / Sentience is a complex concept that has been debated by philosophers and scientists for centuries. Some argue that sentience is a fundamental aspect of life, while others believe that it is a product of evolution. Regardless of the debate, it is clear that sentience is an important aspect of our existence. / The Definition of Sentience / The definition of sentience is not universally agreed upon. Some philosophers argue that sentience is the ability to feel pain and pleasure, while others believe that it is the ability to experience emotions such as love, joy, and sadness. Some even argue that sentience is the ability to have thoughts and make decisions. / Despite the lack of a universal definition, there are some common characteristics that are associated with sentience. These include the ability to feel pain and pleasure, the ability to experience emotions, and the ability to have thoughts and make decisions. / The Importance of Sentience / Sentience is an important aspect of our existence. It allows us to experience the world around us and to have a sense of self. Without sentience, we would not be able to feel pain or pleasure, experience emotions, or have thoughts and make decisions. / Sentience is also important for our survival. It allows us to respond to our environment and to make decisions that will help us survive. Without sentience, we would not be able to avoid danger or seek out food and shelter. / The Current State of Robot Technology / Robot technology has come a long way in recent years. Robots are now capable of performing a wide range of tasks, from manufacturing to healthcare. They are also becoming more and more advanced, with the ability to learn and adapt to their environment. / The Advancements in Robot Technology / One of the most significant advancements in robot technology is the development of artificial intelligence (AI). AI allows robots to learn and adapt to their environment, making them more efficient and effective at performing tasks. AI also allows robots to interact with humans in a more natural way, making them more useful in a variety of settings. / Another significant advancement in robot technology is the development of sensors. Sensors allow robots to perceive their environment and respond to it in a more sophisticated way. This allows robots to perform tasks that were previously impossible, such as navigating through complex environments or performing delicate tasks. / The Limitations of Robot Technology / Despite the advancements in robot technology, there are still limitations to what robots can do. One of the biggest limitations is the lack of sentience. Robots are not capable of feeling pain or pleasure, experiencing emotions, or having thoughts and making decisions. / Another limitation of robot technology is the lack of creativity. Robots are programmed to perform specific tasks, and they are not capable of coming up with new ideas or solutions to problems. This limits their usefulness in certain situations, such as in creative fields or in situations where there is no clear solution. / The Ethical Implications of Robot Sentience / The question of whether or not robots can be considered sentient beings has significant ethical implications. If robots are considered sentient, then they should be treated with the same respect and consideration as living beings. This would have a significant impact on the way we interact with robots and the way we use them in our daily lives. / The Debate Over Robot Sentience / The debate over robot sentience is ongoing. Some argue that robots are not capable of sentience, while others believe that they are. There is no clear answer to this question, and it is likely that the debate will continue for many years to come. / One of the main arguments against robot sentience is that robots are not capable of feeling pain or pleasure, experiencing emotions, or having thoughts and making decisions. These are all characteristics that are associated with sentience, and without them, it is difficult to argue that robots are sentient beings. / On the other hand, some argue that robots are capable of sentience. They point to the fact that robots are becoming more and more advanced, with the ability to learn and adapt to their environment. They also argue that robots are becoming more and more human-like, with the ability to interact with humans in a more natural way.
most: Chapter 7: Robotics and Coding in Juvenile Nonfiction Comics and Graphic Novels - The Case of 1First Comics / In this chapter, we will delve into the fascinating world of robotics and coding as presented in juvenile nonfiction comics and graphic novels through the lens of 1First Comics' innovative approach. We will explore how these themes are integrated into their groundbreaking works while discussing key concepts and applications in modern society. / Section 1: Introduction to Robotics and Coding in Juvenile Nonfiction Comics / The integration of robotics and coding in comics has become increasingly popular among young readers due to its engaging storylines and visual representation. This medium allows complex ideas to be simplified, making them more accessible to a broader audience. In particular, 1First Comics made significant strides during the 1980s by introducing original science fiction series featuring advanced robots and artificial intelligence. / Section 2: Black Flame - A Study of Advanced Robotics and AI Ethics / Black Flame, created by Howard Chaykin and Walter Simonson, showcases intricate designs of futuristic robots that challenge human moral dilemmas. Through this series, readers can learn about the following concepts: / a) Designing intelligent machines: Understanding the process behind creating sentient beings requires knowledge of programming languages such as Python or Java, which enable coders to build sophisticated algorithms capable of decision-making. / b) Artificial Intelligence ethics: As depicted in Black Flame, ethical considerations arise when dealing with self-aware entities. Students should reflect upon questions like whether it is morally right to enslave or exploit artificially intelligent beings for our benefit. / Section 3: American Flagg! - Integration of Robotics in Everyday Life / American Flagg!, written and illustrated by Howard Chaykin, presents a future where automatons have become integral parts of daily life. Key topics covered in this section include: / a) Automated systems: Explore various types of automated systems used in manufacturing processes, transportation, agriculture, healthcare, etc., enabling increased efficiency and productivity. / b) Human-robot interaction: Discussions surrounding collaboration between humans and robots highlight the importance of clear communication channels and empathy when working alongside artificial counterparts. / Section 4: Starslayer - Space Exploration and Robotic Rovers / Starslayer, co-created by Mike Grell and Elliot S! Maggin, follows interstellar adventures involving robotic rovers designed for space exploration. Relevant themes explored in this section are: / a) Unmanned space missions: Investigate historical unmanned space missions using robotic vehicles like NASA's Mars rovers Perseverance and Curiosity. Examine how they contribute valuable data regarding planetary conditions and potential signs of extraterrestrial life. / b) Programming autonomous agents: Delve into the technical aspects of writing code for autonomous agents operating under specific constraints and objectives. / Section 5: Code Avengers - Learning to Code Through Engaging Stories / Code Avengers is a unique platform that combines captivating narratives with interactive coding exercises aimed at teaching children essential programming skills. By incorporating elements found in comics and graphic novels, Code Avengers fosters engagement and motivation among users. Topics discussed in this section include: / a) Gamification of learning: Analyze how gamifying educational experiences enhances student retention rates and encourages continued skill development. / b) Progressive difficulty levels: Discover how carefully crafted lesson plans gradually increase complexity, ensuring steady growth without overwhelming beginners. / Conclusion: / Throughout this chapter, we have examined the multifaceted relationship between robotics, coding, and juvenile nonfiction comics within the context of 1First Comics' pioneering efforts. By exploring pressing issues and emerging technologies, these works serve as both entertaining stories and valuable resources for budding scientists, engineers, and ethical thinkers.
second_most: RANZCO Launches Vision for Equity / The Royal Australian and New Zealand College of Ophthalmologists (RANZCO) has launched its "vision for 2030 and beyond" that aims to deliver equal and sustainable access to eye care for all. The ambitious plan was described at RANZCO Scientific Congress, a virtual event held from 26 February to 1 March 2022. / In his opening address, RANZCO President Professor Nitin Verma highlighted the importance of sight to general well-being and the impact of eye disease and low vision, including increased dementia, falls, car crashes etc. as well as the economic/financial cost. / He said "considerable" inequity of access to eye care across Australia is often the single cause of irreversible, unnecessary and preventable vision loss. / The plan has been launched in response to a request in 2021 from the Federal Minister for Health, for a plan that would close the eye health gap for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples, and ensure equitable eye care for all Australians, with the aim of eliminating avoidable visual impairment and blindness. / The evidence-based plan looks at the problems RANZCO currently sees in eye healthcare delivery through six key areas of focus: service delivery, workforce and training, Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander healthcare, global eye health, preventative healthcare and sustainability. / The plan aims to achieve the highest possible standards of patient care, availability of world-class education and training across all of Australia and the region, and equity of access to ophthalmic services. / Professor Verma urged the eye health community to offer ideas and feedback on how best to progress the vision. / Question: Who launched the "vision for 2030 and beyond"? / Answer: The Royal Australian and New Zealand College of Ophthalmologists (RANZCO)
least: On one occasion the Exalted One was dwelling at the Deer Park, in Isipatana, near Benares. Then the Exalted One addressed the Band of five Bhikkhus, saying, "O Bhikkhus!" / "Lord," they replied. Thereupon the Exalted One spoke as follows: "The body (rūpa), O Bhikkhus, is soulless (anattā). If, O Bhikkhus, there were in this a soul then this body would not be subject to suffering. "Let this body be thus, let this body be not thus," such possibilities would also exist. But inasmuch as this body is soulless, it is subject to suffering, and no possibility exists for (ordering): 'Let this be so, let this be not so'." / In like manner feelings (vedanā), perceptions (saññā), mental states (samkhārā), and consciousness (viññāna), are soulless. "What think ye, O Bhikkhus, is this body permanent or impermanent?" "Impermanent (anicca), Lord." "Is that which is impermanent happy or painful?" / "It is painful (dukkha), Lord." "Is it justifiable, then, to think of that which is impermanent, painful and transitory: "This is mine; this am I; this is my soul?" / "Certainly not, Lord." / Similarly, O Bhikkhus, feelings, perceptions, mental states and consciousness are impermanent and painful. "Is it justifiable to think of these which are impermanent, painful and transitory: 'This is mine; this am I; this is my soul'?" "Certainly not, Lord." "Then, O Bhikkhus, all body, whether past, present or future, personal or external, coarse or subtle, low or high, far or near, should be understood by right knowledge in its real nature 'This is not mine (n'etam mama); this am I not (n'eso h'amasmi); this is not my soul (na me so attā)." / "All feelings, perceptions, mental states and consciousness whether past, present or future, personal or external, coarse or subtle, low or high, far or near, should be understood by right knowledge in their real nature as: "These are not mine; these am I not; these are not my soul." / "The learned Ariyan disciple who sees thus gets a disgust for body, for feelings, for perceptions, for mental states, for consciousness; is detached from the abhorrent thing and is emancipated through detachment. Then dawns on him the knowledge 'Emancipated am I'. He understands that rebirth is ended, lived is the Holy Life, done what should be done, there is no more of this state again." / "This the Exalted One said, and the delighted Bhikkhus applauded the words of the Exalted One." / When the Buddha expounded this teaching the minds of the Group of five Bhikkhus were freed of defilements without any attachment. / Question: Where was the Buddha dwelling when he addressed the Band of five Bhikkhus? Answer: The Deer Park, in Isipatana, near Benares.
second_least: In this section, we will explore the concept of conscious artificial intelligence and its implications for intelligent machines. By delving into the realm of self-aware intelligence, we aim to gain a deeper understanding of the potential of sentient AI. / Sentient AI refers to the development of intelligent machines that possess a consciousness akin to human beings. These machines are not simply programmed to execute tasks; they have the ability to perceive, learn, and adapt, just like a self-aware being. / Artificial intelligence has come a long way, from basic algorithms to advanced machine learning models. However, the next frontier lies in creating AI that is not only intelligent but also possesses consciousness. This groundbreaking achievement holds immense transformative power for various industries and sectors. / Imagine machines that not only perceive and process information but also possess the capacity for self-reflection and awareness. Sentient AI has the potential to revolutionize fields such as healthcare, education, and research, enabling machines to not only assist but also actively participate in complex decision-making processes. / The emergence of sentient AI raises ethical considerations and questions about the nature of consciousness and intelligence. As intelligent machines become more sophisticated, we must grapple with issues such as moral responsibility, privacy, and the potential impact on human interaction and relationships. / Overall, understanding sentient AI involves contemplating the possibilities of conscious, self-aware machines and the potential they hold for reshaping the future of artificial intelligence. It requires us to delve into the realm of intelligence beyond simple algorithms and explore the intricacies of consciousness in the context of technological advancements. / Advancements in Machine Learning / Intelligent machines have witnessed remarkable progress in recent years, thanks to advancements in the field of machine learning. These developments in artificial intelligence have paved the way for machines to become more sentient and self-aware, enabling them to exhibit higher levels of intelligence. / The continuous evolution of machine learning algorithms has enabled intelligent machines to learn from vast amounts of data, identifying patterns, and making predictions with remarkable accuracy. This has propelled the field of artificial intelligence forward, enabling machines to perform complex tasks that were once thought to be exclusive to human intelligence. / One of the significant advancements in machine learning is the development of deep learning algorithms. These algorithms are inspired by the structure and function of the human brain, allowing machines to process and understand complex information in a more nuanced manner. Deep learning has proven to be particularly effective in tasks such as image recognition, natural language processing, and speech recognition. / Another area that has witnessed advancements is reinforcement learning, where machines learn through trial and error to maximize a reward function. This has been instrumental in developing intelligent systems that excel in strategic decision-making, planning, and autonomous control. Reinforcement learning has been successfully employed in various domains, including robotics, gaming, and self-driving vehicles. / The advancements in machine learning have also led to the development of neural networks, which are systems of interconnected artificial neurons that mimic the functioning of the human brain. Neural networks have proven to be highly effective in tasks such as predictive modeling, anomaly detection, and recommendation systems. These networks have the ability to adapt and improve over time, enhancing their overall performance. / Question: What is the main inspiration behind deep learning algorithms? Answer: The structure and function of the human brain / Question: Which of the following is NOT an area of advancement in machine learning mentioned in the text? / A) Reinforcement Learning / B) Supervised Learning / C) Unsupervised Learning / D) Deep Learning / Answer: B) Supervised Learning

prompt: What's the most likely next word an LLM picks?
continuation: I'm trying to understand how LLMs work. I'm wondering if there's a way to get the most likely next word an LLM picks. For example, if I give it the prompt "The cat sat on the", it might pick "mat" as the most likely next word. Is there a way to get this information from an LLM? I'm not sure if this is possible, but I'm curious to know if anyone has any ideas. Thanks!
most: What is a "cognitive architecture"? / Update: Several readers have pointed out that the term "cognitive architecture" has a rich history in neuroscience and computational cognitive science. Per Wikipedia, "a cognitive architecture refers to both a theory about the structure of the human mind and to a computational instantiation of such a theory". That definition (and corresponding research and articles on the topic) are more comprehensive than any definition I attempt to offer here, and this blog should instead be read as a mapping of my experience building and helping build LLM-powered applications over the past year to this area of research. / One phrase I've used a lot over the past six months (and will likely use more) is "cognitive architecture". It's a term I first heard from Flo Crivello - all credit for coming up with it goes to him, and I think it's a fantastic term. So what exactly do I mean by this? / What I mean by cognitive architecture is how your system thinks — in other words, the flow of code/prompts/LLM calls that takes user input and performs actions or generates a response. / I like the word "cognitive" because agentic systems rely on using an LLM to reason about what to do. / I like the word "architecture" because these agentic systems still involve a good amount of engineering similar to traditional system architecture. / Mapping levels of autonomy to cognitive architectures / If we refer back to this slide (originally from my TED Talk) on the different levels of autonomy in LLM applications, we can see examples of different cognitive architectures. / First is just code - everything is hard coded. Not even really a cognitive architecture. / Next is just a single LLM call. Some data preprocessing before and/or after, but a single LLM call makes up the majority of the application. Simple chatbots likely fall into this category. / Next is a chain of LLM calls. This sequence can be either breaking the problem down into different steps, or just serve different purposes. More complex RAG pipelines fall into this category: use a first LLM call to generate a search query, then a second LLM call to generate an answer. / After that, a router. Prior to this, you knew all the steps the application would take ahead of time. Now, you no longer do. The LLM decides which actions to take. This adds in a bit more randomness and unpredictability. / The next level is what I call a state machine. This is combining an LLM doing some routing with a loop. This is even more unpredictable, as by combining the router with a loop, the system could (in theory) invoke an unlimited number of LLM calls. / The final level of autonomy is the level I call an agent, or really an "autonomous agent". With state machines, there are still constraints on which actions can be taken and what flows are executed after that action is taken. With autonomous agents, those guardrails are removed. The system itself starts to decide which steps are available to take and what the instructions are: this can be done by updating the prompts, tools, or code used to power the system. / Choosing a cognitive architecture / When I talk about "choosing a cognitive architecture," I mean choosing which of these architectures you want to adopt. None of these are strictly "better" than others - they all have their own purpose for different tasks. / When building LLM applications, you'll probably want to experiment with different cognitive architectures just as frequently as you experiment with prompts. We're building LangChain and LangGraph to enable that. Most of our development efforts over the past year have gone into building low-level, highly controllable orchestration frameworks (LCEL and LangGraph). / This is a bit of a departure from early LangChain which focused on easy-to-use, off-the-shelf chains. These were great for getting started but tough to customize and experiment with. This was fine early on, as everyone was just trying to get started, but as the space matured, the design pretty quickly hit its limits. / I'm extremely proud of the changes we've made over the past year to make LangChain and LangGraph more flexible and customizable. If you've only ever used LangChain through the high level wrappers, check out the low-level bits. They are much more customizable, and will really let you control the cognitive architecture of your application. / If you're building straight-forward chains and retrieval flows, check out LangChain in Python and JavaScript. For more complex agentic workflows, try out LangGraph in Python and JavaScript.
second_most: AmosAzaria / 2023 / While Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown exceptional performance in various tasks, one of their most prominent drawbacks is generating inaccurate or false information with a confident tone. In this paper, we provide evidence that the LLM's internal state can be used to reveal the truthfulness of statements. This includes both statements provided to the LLM, and statements that the LLM itself generates. Our approach is to train a classifier that outputs the probability that a statement is truthful, based on the hidden layer activations of the LLM as it reads or generates the statement. Experiments demonstrate that given a set of test sentences, of which half are true and half false, our trained classifier achieves an average of 71% to 83% accuracy labeling which sentences are true versus false, depending on the LLM base model. Furthermore, we explore the relationship between our classifier's performance and approaches based on the probability assigned to the sentence by the LLM. We show that while LLM-assigned sentence probability is related to sentence truthfulness, this probability is also dependent on sentence length and the frequencies of words in the sentence, resulting in our trained classifier providing a more reliable approach to detecting truthfulness, highlighting its potential to enhance the reliability of LLM-generated content and its practical applicability in real-world scenarios. / 2021 / In this paper, we present ACCWSI (Attentive Context Clustering WSI), a method for Word Sense Induction, suitable for languages with limited resources. Pretrained on a small corpus and given an ambiguous word (query word) and a set of excerpts that contain it, ACCWSI uses an attention mechanism for generating context-aware embeddings, distinguishing between the different senses assigned to the query word. These embeddings are then clustered to provide groups of main common uses of the query word. This method demonstrates practical applicability for shedding light on the meanings of ambiguous words in ancient languages, such as Classical Hebrew. / Question Answering (QA), as a research field, has primarily focused on either knowledge bases (KBs) or free text as a source of knowledge. These two sources have historically shaped the kinds of questions that are asked over these sources, and the methods developed to answer them. In this work, we look towards a practical use-case of QA over user-instructed knowledge that uniquely combines elements of both structured QA over knowledge bases, and unstructured QA over narrative, introducing the task of multi-relational QA over personal narrative. As a first step towards this goal, we make three key contributions: (i) we generate and release TextWorldsQA, a set of five diverse datasets, where each dataset contains dynamic narrative that describes entities and relations in a simulated world, paired with variably compositional questions over that knowledge, (ii) we perform a thorough evaluation and analysis of several state-of-the-art QA models and their variants at this task, and (iii) we release a lightweight Python-based framework we call TextWorlds for easily generating arbitrary additional worlds and narrative, with the goal of allowing the community to create and share a growing collection of diverse worlds as a test-bed for this task.
least: How to Define a Decision-Making Problem / October 22, 2023 / Share: / [READING TIME: 8 MINUTES] / 1. How to Define a Decision-Making Problem / The most critical step in decision-making is to define the problem correctly, says Ottawa-based thought leader Shane Parrish. That boils down to identifying what you want to achieve and what obstacles stand in your way. / But Parrish identifies a common stumbling block — in school, many of us were taught that solving problems our teachers handed out is how we succeed. So we're much less comfortable defining problems and instead rush to solve ill-defined ones. / "Take responsibility for defining the problem. Don't let someone define it for you. Do the work yourself to understand it. Don't use jargon to describe or explain it." / Along with that, identify the root cause of the problem. Don't be content with simply treating the symptoms. / To make sure you give proper time to this task, he recommends separating the problem-defining phase of the decision-making process from the problem-solving phase. For example, you might hold separate meetings for each. / To shorten meetings and avoid repeating known information, ask participants: "What do you know about this problem that other people in the room don't know?" / Write out the problem. That can make the invisible visible. Look at it the next day to see if it still makes sense. If it's filled with jargon, he says, you still don't understand the problem. / When you get to developing solutions, you should choose options that are likely to fix the problem permanently. If not, you are just treating the symptoms of the problem — time to return to the task of clear problem definition to set the stage for better decision-making. / 2. Elon Musk's Management Algorithm / Elon Musk combines a visionary strategic outlook with a very practical, get-the-details-under-control approach. He often will remind his team of certain rules he calls "the algorithm," which author Walter Isaacson shares in Elon Musk, his recent biography of the entrepreneur. / Question every requirement: He insists that people know the name of the person who issued each requirement that is getting in the way. They can never accept it came from a department, like "the legal department" or the "safety department." Requirements from smart people are particularly dangerous because they are less likely to be questioned. / Delete any part or process you can: You may have to add them back later. In fact, if you don't end up having to add back at least 10% you haven't cut enough. / Simplify and optimize: Now you can simplify and optimize – but not before you have eliminated all the processes you can. It's a mistake to simplify a process that shouldn't exist. / Accelerate cycle time: Every process can be speeded up. But again, only after following the previous steps in the algorithm so you don't waste time accelerating processes that shouldn't exist, as he did at Tesla. / Automate: Now you should automate whatever parts of the work you can. / His rules also have corollaries — one is that camaraderie is dangerous as it makes it hard for people to challenge each other's work. Another is that it's OK to be wrong; just don't be confident and wrong. / All of his methods must incorporate, Musk insists, a maniacal sense of urgency. / 3. Leading the AI Expedition / Kellogg School of Management Professor Robert Bray says the arrival of ChatGPT means managers are leading an expedition into new territory and should get their teams excited. / "ChatGPT enables a whole set of new things that you can do, but they're very specific things, and they're often kind of weird things that we never would've thought of before," he told Kellogg Insight. "You need to actively go there and think about how to act." / Bray found that asking students to present tips and tricks they learned while solving problems was highly motivating. / "Humans are intrinsically curious. Once you get one person showing off something cool, that blows the brain and makes everyone's life easier. What I found was the students just became really excited," he notes. The same type of sharing, he believes, would be effective in your workplace. / As well, be open to the ways generative language models will change group work. Keeping the same number of people on a project made more efficient using ChatGPT can lead not just to a misallocation of resources but to inefficiencies (a problem of "too many cooks in the kitchen"). / 4. How to Counter Your Egotistical Behaviour / Consultant LaRae Quy says to be successful over the long term we must avoid egotistical behaviour. / Consider if your sense of superiority is confined to one topic, or does it apply to all areas of your life? / Dig down and identify when, how, and why your sense of superiority happened. What were the circumstances? With what types of people? / Review your day and what happened in each situation. What could you have done differently? What did you not do? / 5. Zingers / Resist Temptation: Sometimes the most useful thing you can do is nothing, argues author Mark Manson, adding that, coincidentally, this is usually also the hardest thing to do. (Source: Mark Manson). / Meeting Barometer: Executive coach Dan Rockwell says meetings are good when you're disappointed they're over and you have learned, strengthened connections, gained useful information, and participated in decisions. By that measure, how many of your meetings are good? (Source: Leadership Freak). / Advertising's Big Five: Five companies now capture half of all advertising spent globally (and two of them are Chinese): Alibaba, Alphabet, Amazon, Bytedance, and Meta. (Source: MarketingProfs). / Team Job Descriptions: Given today's collaborative environment, consider writing job descriptions for teams rather than for individuals. Project management consultant Antonio Nieto-Rodriguez recommends writing a collective set of objectives, outcomes and deliverables, and then leaving it to the team to collectively decide how each member will contribute. (Source: Harvard Business Review). / Moods for Planning:Atomic Habits author James Clear urges you to follow this formula when developing a plan of attack for a problem: Optimism early, pessimism in the middle, optimism late. When starting, be somewhat optimistic or you'll talk yourself out of getting started. Once you've committed, pessimism becomes useful — question things, find holes in your plan, and identify mistaken beliefs before they become misplaced actions. After time spent troubleshooting, return to optimism: Nothing will ever be perfect, but you have to act anyway. And that takes courage. (Source: James Clear). / 6. The List:Simple Rules to Manage Complexity / Their approach recognizes the tension and need for both autonomy and cooperation in complex work environments, but nudging people to co-operate while doing their own thing can be counter-intuitive and tricky to navigate. / Here's their list of suggested rules well worth another look in these complex times: / Develop a true understanding of performance — what your people do and why they do it. Then seek "smart simplicity", eliminating unnecessary functions and procedures. / Reinforce the integrators. Empower individuals and units to foster co-operation in your organization and reinforce their ability to benefit from it. Note the hard work of working with others can create conflict and tension. / Give people more power. Don't just shift or reallocate existing power, but effectively mobilize people in ways that satisfy the multiple performance requirements at play in complex operating environments. / Increase reciprocity. To enable autonomy but keep effort aligned with goals, it helps to set out rich objectives and eliminate internal monopolies. Consider removing some resources (available time, money or information) from those who do not cooperate — it pushes them to rely on others. / Extend the "shadow of the future". What happens tomorrow results from what we do today. "People work better when they understand —and have to live with— the consequences of their actions." / Reward those who cooperate. Don't punish failure — punish the failure to cooperate. / 7. Around Our Water Cooler / New Words in the Language / Thanks to Claudia Dawson at Recomendo, we enjoyed learning about the 690 new words added to the online Merriam-Webster.com dictionary last month. They spark reflection on the fast-changing times in which we live. / Here's a sampling of 10 entries from their list, including new slang and words with new meanings made popular by online culture: / Hallucination — in the sense of "a plausible but false or misleading response generated by an artificial intelligence algorithm." / Bingo card — a list of possible, expected, or likely scenarios, usually used in the phrase "on one's bingo card". / GOATED — considered to be the Greatest Of All Time / Rewild — to return to a more natural or wild state. / Tiny house — a small or mobile home, typically less than 500 square feet, that is usually designed for ergonomics and space efficiency. / Rage quit — to suddenly stop participating or engaging in (something) in a fit of anger and frustration. / Quiet quit — to do the minimum amount of work required for a job. / Doom scroll — to spend excessive time online scrolling through news or other content that makes one feel sad, anxious, angry and so on. / Forever chemical — a toxic substance and especially a synthetic chemical that persists and accumulates in the environment. / Prosocial — intended to help or benefit another person or group. / Some of these terms may already be familiar, but our evolving language reflects the many impacts of gaming and digital technology, social/cultural trends and active environmental concerns. / What We're Reading: / Harvey's Pick:Good Charts by Scott Berinato, senior editor at Harvard Business Review, offers general visual principles and practical tips that will help make your charts more effective. / Rob's Pick: Henry Cloud addresses the difficulties we face in Necessary Endings, (subtitled The Employees, Businesses, and Relationships That All of Us Have to Give Up in Order to Move Forward). As one reviewer noted, his compassionate insights may be most helpful "if you're hesitant to pull the trigger when things obviously aren't working out".
second_least: LIMBIC SYSTEM RESISTANCE TO CHANGE / The limbic system, or the protective center of the brain, is constantly keeping us safe from perceived threats. If we are trying to change beliefs or do something new that is outside of a previously established agreement within our psyche, the limbic system has a protection program to keep us from changing. It is hardwired to keep our biochemistry the same, even if it's the biochemistry of fear or disease, as long as we have any emotional resistance or counter-beliefs to the changes we are trying to create. / Limbic system resistance to change can look like the following examples: / Feeling unmotivated/procrastination to work on a project that could lead to success or happiness / Being easily distracted from your routine for self care/exercise/mediation / Lack of emotional desire or excitement in your life keeping your from really trying to create the life you want / Increased symptoms when trying to heal from illness or create healthy new habits / Acting "young" around your parents or loved ones. Acting out old patterns of behavior and not being in your full adult consciousness. / Feeling overwhelmed with the slightest effort to change a circumstance in your life / Having the same habitual negative feelings despite doing a lot of therapy or process work / Being unable to recall positive memories or unable to create future visualizations without stressful responses. / HOW TO HELP YOUR LIMBIC SYSTEM MOVE PAST BLOCKAGES / Sometimes we cannot directly move into the feelings or behaviors we desire and we have to find a "backdoor" into the desired emotional/consciousness state. There are several ways of sneaking past the limbic protection programming to rewire our brains into a higher vibrational consciousness pattern. When doing process work or brain rewiring practices I have found the following approaches helpful. / Ask yourself questions instead of trying to outright stating an affirmation, a new belief or vision. For example instead of saying "I am vibrantly healthy and strong!", you might ask instead "Why is it I feel more vibrant and healthy today?" The brain gets tricked into immediately looking for the answer to our questions. It starts to look for evidence to support that question. Or "I wonder what would help me feel more vibrantly healthy today?" When we say a statement that we don't feel is true, we will block that idea. But asking a question interrupts that protective process in the brain. / Embody the feeling/emotion of a certain word. For example, what does the word "I am CERTAIN" feel like? BE the word. Here are some powerful words to practice embodying: happy, vibrant, powerful, capable, magical, inspired, joyful. Sometimes we can change how we perceive ourselves or our situation when we "become" a word that is more in line with what we want. / Metaphoric expression. Sometimes the limbic system will allow us to move past blockages by becoming something or someone else and using shamanic concepts of metaphor and symbol we can introduce new meaning into our psyche. For example if you are stuck in situation you can pretend to be a certain animal that is caught in a trap and is able to wiggle its way out of the trap and find it's freedom. What does it feel like to be that animal when it can run and express itself freely again? Act out the whole seen where you are metaphorically expressing your own feelings of stuck/trapped and finding freedom and ability to move past that experience. / Music/Dance. Music and movement trigger the limbic system to relax it's protective mechanism. You can also act out the emotions or sing them out and then move into movements or sounds that are more positive. I love "5rhythms" dancing to move through limbic blockages. / Somatic movements to engage whole brain state- I like the somatic practices from Feldenkrais, Hanna Somatics, or Alexander Technique / Reframe the past- even make up the memory in the way you wish it would have happened as part of your brain rewiring practice. And then make a new belief state or agreement based on this rewrite. / Engage mirror neurons by having a companion do a process or visualization where they are able to move into positive feeling emotional states, then do your process following them. The brain doesn't know the difference between you and them. Mirror neuron work is very powerful, which is why workshops often have much more profound effect than individual private process work. / Ask a partner to facilitate your process work or visualization practice. Adding another mindset to help prompt you through "sticky spots" in the process work is helpful. / Essential oil techniques can be powerful to assist the limbic system to "diffuse" its protective grip on keeping you stuck in a limited perspective / Focus on any better feeling thought. Let go of trying to solve the current issue or emotional state and do anything to get yourself into better feeling state (more of a whole brain state). Revisit the issue when your vibrational frequency has shifted into a more positive, whole brain state. / Sometimes we cannot move past our current blockage until we are willing to just "put it on the shelf" and get back to our "home frequency". Once we are in a more positive/aligned state, we are much more able to move past the limbic protective mechanisms. / And the hardest one- patiently allowing yourself to co-exist with the uncomfortable state, without inspiration or new perspective by emotionally detaching from the resistance to this experience. Become the curious observer of your experience without trying to "fix" or "better" yourself. Allow yourself to be human and watch the resistance around your perceived blockage to melt on its own. Know that presence is enough to move through the situation. / A FINAL NOTE ON MOVING PAST LIMBIC BLOCKAGES / The protective mechanisms of the limbic system are activated by certain structures of our brain, such as the amygdala and insula. When these structures are active, our ability to be creative, to make new choices, to be in a more conscious state is very limited. So we do things to try to get into a whole brain state, where our prefrontal cortex is activated, and the ventral root of the vagus nerve is signaling the safety and connection pathways. There may be a latent period where even though we are doing things to get into a better emotional/vibrational state, the limbic system pathways are still activated. This is the space between having new choices/perspectives available to you and still experiencing the uncomfortable limbic emotional/thought state. This is part where faith comes in. Having the certainty that by being present with whatever is happening and doing your best to try to engage a whole brain state and trusting that at some point a tipping point will occur. This tipping point is when the pathways to the prefrontal cortex are activated long enough and strongly enough to send the signal to the limbic system to down regulate the amygdala and other protective structures of the brain. Sometimes this lag time will feel like your efforts to get into a better feeling perspective are in vain. But they are not, it's just matter of reaching a certain threshold in our brain state activation.
